title: HTML Mastery Bible
subtitle: Semantic tags, SEO, forms & native APIs

sections.intro: HTML is the skeleton of every web page. Semantic HTML is the foundation of accessibility, SEO, and maintainable code.

cards.title: HTML5 Document Structure
cards.contentHtml:
Every HTML document follows this structure. Understanding each part is crucial.

<!DOCTYPE html>                <!-- Tells browser: use HTML5 standards mode -->
<html lang="en">                  <!-- Root element. lang= helps screen readers -->
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">          <!-- Character encoding (ALWAYS first) -->
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <meta name="description" content="Page description for SEO">
  <title>Page Title</title>        <!-- Shows in tab, critical for SEO -->
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">
  <link rel="icon" href="/favicon.ico">
  <link rel="canonical" href="https://example.com/page">
  
  <!-- Open Graph (social sharing) -->
  <meta property="og:title" content="Title">
  <meta property="og:image" content="https://example.com/og.png">
  
  <!-- Preload critical resources -->
  <link rel="preload" href="font.woff2" as="font" crossorigin>
  <link rel="preconnect" href="https://api.example.com">
</head>
<body>
  <header>...</header>
  <main>...</main>
  <footer>...</footer>
  <script src="app.js" defer></script> <!-- defer = load after HTML parsed -->
</body>
</html>
defer vs async: defer loads JS in parallel but executes AFTER HTML is parsed (in order). async loads in parallel and executes immediately when ready (out of order). Use defer for your app bundle, async for analytics scripts.

cards.title: Semantic HTML — Every Element
cards.contentHtml:
Semantic elements describe their meaning, not just appearance. Critical for accessibility and SEO.

Page Structure

<header>    <!-- Site/section header. Can contain nav, logo, search -->
<nav>       <!-- Navigation links. Use aria-label for multiple navs -->
<main>      <!-- Primary content. ONE per page. Skip-link target -->
<section>   <!-- Thematic grouping WITH a heading -->
<article>   <!-- Self-contained content (blog post, comment, card) -->
<aside>     <!-- Tangentially related (sidebar, pull quote, ad) -->
<footer>    <!-- Site/section footer. Copyright, links -->
<figure>    <!-- Self-contained media with caption -->
<figcaption> <!-- Caption for figure -->
<details>   <!-- Expandable/collapsible content (no JS needed!) -->
<summary>   <!-- Visible heading for details -->
<dialog>    <!-- Native modal dialog. showModal() / close() -->
<time>      <!-- Machine-readable date: <time datetime="2026-04-06"> -->
<mark>      <!-- Highlighted/relevant text -->
<abbr>      <!-- Abbreviation: <abbr title="HyperText...">HTML</abbr> -->
<address>   <!-- Contact information for nearest article/body -->
div and span have NO semantic meaning. They're for styling/grouping only. If you can replace a div with a semantic element (article, section, nav), always do it. Screen readers announce semantic elements — they skip divs.

cards.title: Forms — Complete Reference
cards.contentHtml:
<form action="/submit" method="post" novalidate>
  <!-- Text inputs -->
  <label for="email">Email</label>
  <input type="email" id="email" name="email" required 
         placeholder="you@example.com"
         autocomplete="email"
         pattern="[^@]+@[^@]+\.[^@]+">
  
  <!-- ALL input types -->
  <input type="text">       <input type="password">
  <input type="number" min="0" max="100" step="1">
  <input type="tel">        <input type="url">
  <input type="search">     <input type="date">
  <input type="time">       <input type="datetime-local">
  <input type="color">      <input type="range">
  <input type="file" accept="image/*" multiple>
  <input type="hidden" name="csrf" value="token">
  
  <!-- Select, Textarea, Datalist -->
  <select name="country">
    <optgroup label="Europe">
      <option value="uk">United Kingdom</option>
    </optgroup>
  </select>
  
  <textarea rows="4" maxlength="500"></textarea>
  
  <input list="fruits">
  <datalist id="fruits">
    <option value="Apple">
  </datalist>
  
  <!-- Validation attributes -->
  required  minlength  maxlength  min  max  pattern  step
  
  <button type="submit">Send</button>
</form>
Always use <label> with for= matching input id=. Clicking the label focuses the input. This is essential for accessibility AND improves mobile UX (bigger tap target).

cards.title: Accessibility (a11y) — Critical Knowledge
cards.contentHtml:
Accessibility isn't optional — it's required by law in many countries (ADA, EAA, WCAG).

ARIA Attributes

<!-- Role: what the element IS -->
role="button" role="navigation" role="dialog" role="alert"
role="tab" role="tabpanel" role="tablist"
role="progressbar" role="status" role="search"

<!-- State: current condition -->
aria-expanded="true|false"     <!-- dropdown open/closed -->
aria-selected="true"          <!-- tab/option selected -->
aria-hidden="true"            <!-- hidden from screen readers -->
aria-disabled="true"          <!-- not interactive -->
aria-checked="true"           <!-- checkbox/toggle state -->
aria-current="page"           <!-- current page in nav -->

<!-- Properties: relationships -->
aria-label="Close menu"       <!-- accessible name (no visible text) -->
aria-labelledby="heading-id" <!-- references another element as label -->
aria-describedby="help-id"   <!-- references description element -->
aria-live="polite|assertive"  <!-- announce dynamic changes -->
aria-controls="panel-id"     <!-- this controls that element -->

Focus Management

<!-- Skip link (first element in body) -->
<a href="#main" class="skip-link">Skip to content</a>

<!-- tabindex -->
tabindex="0"    <!-- Add to tab order (for custom interactive elements) -->
tabindex="-1"   <!-- Focusable via JS only (for focus management) -->
<!-- NEVER use tabindex > 0 — breaks natural tab order -->

<!-- Focus trap for modals -->
<!-- Trap focus inside dialog: first/last focusable elements -->
<!-- Use inert attribute on background content -->
<div inert>Background content</div>
First rule of ARIA: Don't use ARIA if a native HTML element does the job. <button> is better than <div role='button' tabindex='0'>. Native elements have built-in keyboard handling, focus management, and screen reader support.

cards.title: HTML APIs You Should Know
cards.contentHtml:
Native Dialog

<dialog id="modal">
  <h2>Confirm</h2>
  <p>Are you sure?</p>
  <form method="dialog">  <!-- auto-closes dialog -->
    <button value="cancel">Cancel</button>
    <button value="confirm">OK</button>
  </form>
</dialog>
// modal.showModal(); — opens with backdrop, focus trap, Escape to close
// modal.close(); — closes programmatically
// modal.returnValue — which button was clicked

Other HTML APIs

Popover API: popover attribute + popovertarget — native tooltips/dropdowns without JS. View Transitions API: document.startViewTransition() — smooth page transitions. Intersection Observer: lazy loading, infinite scroll, scroll animations. Web Components: Custom Elements + Shadow DOM + Templates — framework-agnostic components.

cards.title: SEO Essentials in HTML
cards.contentHtml:
Search engines read your HTML to understand your content. Structure matters.

Critical SEO Elements

Title tag: 50-60 chars, unique per page, primary keyword first. Meta description: 150-160 chars, compelling, includes keywords. Heading hierarchy: One h1 per page, h2-h6 in order (never skip levels). Canonical URL: prevents duplicate content penalties. Alt text: describe images for screen readers AND Google Image Search. Structured data: JSON-LD for rich snippets.

<!-- Structured Data (JSON-LD) -->
<script type="application/ld+json">
{{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "Article",
  "headline": "Article Title",
  "author": {{ "@type": "Person", "name": "Author" }},
  "datePublished": "2026-04-06"
}}
</script>

sections.intro: Tables, lists, and content elements — the building blocks of structured data display.

cards.title: Tables — Complete Accessible Reference
cards.contentHtml:
<table>
  <caption>Quarterly Revenue</caption>  <!-- Visible title, screen reader announcement -->
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th scope="col">Quarter</th>  <!-- scope tells SR this heads a column -->
      <th scope="col">Revenue</th>
      <th scope="col">Growth</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <th scope="row">Q1</th>  <!-- Row header -->
      <td>$1.2M</td>
      <td>+12%</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
  <tfoot>
    <tr><th scope="row">Total</th><td colspan="2">$4.8M</td></tr>
  </tfoot>
</table>
Always use <caption> and scope attributes. Screen readers use these to announce 'Revenue column, Q1 row: $1.2M' — without them, tables are incomprehensible to blind users.

cards.title: Lists — Ordered, Unordered, Description
cards.contentHtml:
<!-- Unordered list (bullets) -->
<ul>
  <li>Item one</li>
  <li>Item two
    <ul><li>Nested item</li></ul>  <!-- Nested list INSIDE li -->
  </li>
</ul>

<!-- Ordered list (numbers) -->
<ol type="1" start="5" reversed>
  <li value="10">Custom numbering</li>
</ol>

<!-- Description list (key-value pairs, glossary, metadata) -->
<dl>
  <dt>HTML</dt>
  <dd>HyperText Markup Language</dd>
  <dt>CSS</dt>
  <dd>Cascading Style Sheets</dd>
</dl>
Use <dl> for FAQ pages, metadata displays, settings panels — any key-value layout. It's more semantic than a table for term/description pairs.

cards.title: Details & Summary — Native Accordion
cards.contentHtml:
<!-- No JavaScript needed! -->
<details>
  <summary>What is your refund policy?</summary>
  <p>Full refund within 30 days of purchase...</p>
</details>

<!-- Exclusive accordion (name attribute, one open at a time) -->
<details name="faq" open>
  <summary>Question 1</summary>
  <p>Answer 1</p>
</details>
<details name="faq">
  <summary>Question 2</summary>
  <p>Answer 2</p>
</details>
The name attribute (2024) creates exclusive accordions — opening one closes others in the same group. Zero JavaScript. Perfect for FAQ pages.

sections.intro: Images, video, responsive media, and performance optimization.

cards.title: Images — Complete Guide
cards.contentHtml:
<!-- Responsive image with art direction -->
<picture>
  <source media="(min-width: 800px)" srcset="hero-wide.avif" type="image/avif">
  <source media="(min-width: 800px)" srcset="hero-wide.webp" type="image/webp">
  <source srcset="hero-mobile.avif" type="image/avif">
  <source srcset="hero-mobile.webp" type="image/webp">
  <img src="hero-fallback.jpg" alt="Description" 
       width="1200" height="600"
       loading="lazy"
       decoding="async"
       fetchpriority="high">  <!-- for hero/LCP image -->
</picture>

<!-- Responsive srcset (resolution switching) -->
<img srcset="img-320.jpg 320w, img-640.jpg 640w, img-1200.jpg 1200w"
     sizes="(max-width: 640px) 100vw, 50vw"
     src="img-640.jpg" alt="...">
loading='lazy' defers offscreen images. fetchpriority='high' on your LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) image tells the browser to prioritize it. Always set width and height to prevent layout shift (CLS).

cards.title: Video, Audio & Embeds
cards.contentHtml:
<video controls preload="metadata" poster="thumb.jpg"
       width="640" height="360">
  <source src="video.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  <source src="video.webm" type="video/webm">
  <track kind="subtitles" src="subs-en.vtt" srclang="en" label="English" default>
  Your browser doesn't support video.
</video>

<!-- Responsive iframe -->
<div style="aspect-ratio: 16/9">
  <iframe src="..." style="width:100%;height:100%"
          loading="lazy" allow="fullscreen"></iframe>
</div>

cards.title: Web Components
cards.contentHtml:
// Define a custom element
class MyCard extends HTMLElement {{
  constructor() {{
    super();
    this.attachShadow({{ mode: 'open' }});
    this.shadowRoot.innerHTML = `
      <style>:host {{ display: block; border: 1px solid #ccc; padding: 1rem; }}</style>
      <h2><slot name="title"></slot></h2>
      <slot></slot>
    `;
  }}
  static get observedAttributes() {{ return ['variant']; }}
  attributeChangedCallback(name, old, val) {{ /* react to changes */ }}
}}
customElements.define('my-card', MyCard);

// Usage:
// <my-card><span slot="title">Hello</span>Content here</my-card>
Web Components are framework-agnostic. They work in React, Angular, Vue, or plain HTML. Use them for: design system primitives that must work across teams/frameworks, embeddable widgets, micro-frontends.

sections.intro: Advanced HTML — APIs, Web Components, and modern capabilities.

cards.title: Intersection Observer API
cards.contentHtml:
// Lazy load images when they enter viewport
const observer = new IntersectionObserver((entries) => {{
  entries.forEach(entry => {{
    if (entry.isIntersecting) {{
      const img = entry.target;
      img.src = img.dataset.src;   // Load real image
      observer.unobserve(img);      // Stop observing
    }}
  }});
}}, {{
  root: null,          // viewport
  rootMargin: '200px',  // load 200px before visible
  threshold: 0          // trigger when any pixel visible
}});

document.querySelectorAll('img[data-src]').forEach(img => observer.observe(img));

Other uses: infinite scroll triggers, scroll-based animations, impression tracking (ads), sticky header detection, lazy-loading components.

cards.title: Resize Observer & Mutation Observer
cards.contentHtml:
// Resize Observer — watch element size changes
const ro = new ResizeObserver(entries => {{
  for (const entry of entries) {{
    const {{ width, height }} = entry.contentRect;
    entry.target.classList.toggle('compact', width < 300);
  }}
}});
ro.observe(element);
// Use case: container-query-like behavior in JS

// Mutation Observer — watch DOM changes
const mo = new MutationObserver(mutations => {{
  mutations.forEach(m => {{
    if (m.type === 'childList') console.log('Children changed');
    if (m.type === 'attributes') console.log('Attr changed:', m.attributeName);
  }});
}});
mo.observe(element, {{ childList: true, attributes: true, subtree: true }});

cards.title: Web Storage & IndexedDB
cards.contentHtml:
// localStorage: persists forever, 5-10MB, sync
localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark');
localStorage.getItem('theme');  // 'dark'
localStorage.removeItem('theme');

// sessionStorage: same API, cleared on tab close

// IndexedDB: large structured data, async, indexes
const request = indexedDB.open('myDB', 1);
request.onupgradeneeded = (e) => {{
  const db = e.target.result;
  const store = db.createObjectStore('users', {{ keyPath: 'id' }});
  store.createIndex('email', 'email', {{ unique: true }});
}};
// Use Dexie.js wrapper for cleaner API

// Cache API: for caching HTTP responses (Service Worker)
const cache = await caches.open('v1');
await cache.put(request, response);
const cached = await cache.match(request);

cards.title: Drag and Drop API
cards.contentHtml:
<!-- Draggable element -->
<div draggable="true"
     ondragstart="event.dataTransfer.setData('text/plain', this.id)">
  Drag me
</div>

<!-- Drop zone -->
<div ondragover="event.preventDefault()"
     ondrop="handleDrop(event)">
  Drop here
</div>

// Modern approach: use libraries
// @dnd-kit (React), SortableJS, or CDK drag-drop (Angular)
// Native API is functional but has many quirks

sections.intro: HTML performance, resource hints, and page load optimization.

cards.title: Resource Hints — Preload, Prefetch, Preconnect
cards.contentHtml:
<!-- PRELOAD: download NOW, needed for current page -->
<link rel="preload" href="critical.css" as="style">
<link rel="preload" href="hero.avif" as="image" fetchpriority="high">
<link rel="preload" href="font.woff2" as="font" type="font/woff2" crossorigin>

<!-- PREFETCH: download later, needed for NEXT page -->
<link rel="prefetch" href="/about">
<link rel="prefetch" href="next-page.js" as="script">

<!-- PRECONNECT: establish connection early (DNS + TCP + TLS) -->
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://api.example.com">
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" crossorigin>

<!-- DNS-PREFETCH: only DNS lookup (less than preconnect) -->
<link rel="dns-prefetch" href="https://analytics.example.com">

<!-- MODULEPRELOAD: preload ES modules -->
<link rel="modulepreload" href="app.js">
Priority hints: fetchpriority='high' on your LCP image/above-fold resources. fetchpriority='low' on below-fold images. Browsers already prioritize, but hints override when you know better.

cards.title: Script Loading Strategies
cards.contentHtml:
<!-- BLOCKING (default): stops HTML parsing -->
<script src="app.js"></script>

<!-- DEFER: download in parallel, execute AFTER HTML parsed, IN ORDER -->
<script src="app.js" defer></script>
<!-- Best for: your app bundle, anything that needs DOM ready -->

<!-- ASYNC: download in parallel, execute IMMEDIATELY when ready, NO ORDER -->
<script src="analytics.js" async></script>
<!-- Best for: independent scripts (analytics, ads, widgets) -->

<!-- MODULE: deferred by default, strict mode, import/export -->
<script type="module" src="app.js"></script>

<!-- INLINE critical: theme/font flash prevention -->
<script>
  document.documentElement.dataset.theme =
    localStorage.getItem('theme') || 'system';
</script>
defer scripts execute in order. async scripts execute in download-completion order (unpredictable). If script B depends on script A, use defer on both — async might run B before A.

cards.title: Meta Tags — Complete Reference
cards.contentHtml:
<!-- Essential -->
<meta charset="UTF-8">                          <!-- ALWAYS first -->
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
<title>Page Title (50-60 chars)</title>
<meta name="description" content="150-160 chars">

<!-- SEO -->
<link rel="canonical" href="https://example.com/page">
<meta name="robots" content="index, follow">
<meta name="robots" content="noindex, nofollow">  <!-- block crawling -->

<!-- Open Graph (Facebook, LinkedIn, iMessage) -->
<meta property="og:title" content="Page Title">
<meta property="og:description" content="Description">
<meta property="og:image" content="https://example.com/og.png">
<meta property="og:url" content="https://example.com/page">
<meta property="og:type" content="website">

<!-- Twitter Card -->
<meta name="twitter:card" content="summary_large_image">
<meta name="twitter:title" content="Title">

<!-- Security -->
<meta http-equiv="Content-Security-Policy" content="default-src 'self'">
<meta http-equiv="X-Content-Type-Options" content="nosniff">

<!-- PWA -->
<link rel="manifest" href="/manifest.json">
<meta name="theme-color" content="#6366f1">
<meta name="apple-mobile-web-app-capable" content="yes">

cards.title: HTML Performance Checklist
cards.contentHtml:
1. Minimize render-blocking resources: Inline critical CSS, defer JS, preload fonts.

2. Optimize images: AVIF/WebP, responsive srcset, lazy loading, width/height attributes (prevent CLS), fetchpriority on LCP image.

3. Reduce HTML size: Remove unnecessary wrappers (use semantic elements), minify in production, enable Brotli/gzip compression.

4. Resource hints: Preconnect to API/CDN origins, preload critical resources, prefetch next-page assets.

5. Font optimization: font-display: swap, preload the WOFF2 file, subset fonts (only needed characters), self-host instead of Google Fonts CDN.

6. Third-party scripts: Load with async, defer non-critical (analytics, chat widgets) until after page load. Use loading='lazy' on iframes.

7. Reduce DOM size: Keep under 1,500 elements. Deep nesting hurts performance. Virtualize long lists. Use content-visibility: auto for offscreen sections.

Run Lighthouse on every PR. Set performance budgets: bundle < 200KB gzipped, LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1. Failing the budget blocks the deploy.

sections.intro: Building the web for everyone. Accessibility is not an afterthought; it is fundamental to modern markup.

cards.title: Semantic HTML Boundaries
cards.contentHtml:
Semantic HTML natively provides meaning and keyboard functionality that plain `<div>`s lack.

  • <main>: The dominant content of the page. Only one visible per document.
  • <article> vs <section>: An `article` is independently distributable (a blog post). A `section` groups related content structurally but isn't necessarily standalone.
  • <nav>: Wrap major navigation blocks. Provide an `aria-label` like "Main" or "Footer" if multiple exist.
  • <button> vs <a>: `<a href>` navigates. `<button>` performs an action on the page. Never use `<div onClick>` without adding `tabindex="0"`, `role="button"`, and handling the `Enter/Space` keys manually.

cards.title: ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications)
cards.contentHtml:
ARIA bridges the gap for complex interactive UI (tabs, modals, comboboxes) where native HTML falls short.

  • First Rule of ARIA: No ARIA is better than bad ARIA. Native elements are always preferred.
  • aria-hidden="true": Hides decorative elements (like SVG icons) from screen readers.
  • aria-label / aria-labelledby: Provides an invisible text label for icon-only buttons (like a search magnifying glass).
  • aria-live: Used on dynamically updated areas (e.g. error messages, toast notifications). Screen readers will interrupt and read `"assertive"` updates immediately, or wait for pauses for `"polite"` updates.

sections.intro: Recent CSS evolutions drastically reduce the need for preprocessors and complex JS logic.

cards.title: Game-Changing Selectors
cards.contentHtml:
• :has() (The Parent Selector): Selects an element if it contains a certain descendent. Ex: `figure:has(figcaption)` selects only figures that have captions. It completely changes CSS architecture.
  • :is() and :where(): Groups selectors succinctly. `:is(h1, h2, h3) span` instead of writing them all out. `:where()` is identical but has zero specificity, perfect for library resets.
  • Native Nesting: CSS now natively supports standard nesting (similar to SCSS/LESS) without build tools. `nav { ul { padding: 0; } }`

cards.title: Layout & Responsiveness
cards.contentHtml:
• Container Queries (@container): Instead of styling based on viewport width (`@media`), style based on the parent wrapper's width. This allows reusable components (like cards) to look totally different inside a tight sidebar vs a massive main grid.
  • CSS Subgrid: Allows nested elements to perfectly align to their parent's grid tracks. If a Card has a Grid layout, `grid-template-rows: subgrid` allows headers and text across multiple sibling cards to perfectly align with each other.
  • text-wrap: balance / pretty: Improves typography natively. `balance` prevents massive headlines with a single "widow" word on the final line by mathematically balancing the text block width.

sections.intro: CSS Anchor Positioning, View Transitions, Cascade Layers, and the future of native layout.

cards.title: Anchor Positioning API
cards.contentHtml:
Position elements relative to any other "anchor" without shared parents or JS libraries:

.anchor { anchor-name: --my-btn; }

.popover {
  position: absolute;
  position-anchor: --my-btn;
  bottom: anchor(top);
  left: anchor(center);
}
  • Native Popovers: Combined with `popover` attribute for top-layer rendering.
  • Fallback Logic: Use `position-try-options` for safe repositioning.

cards.title: View Transitions API
cards.contentHtml:
Seamlessly animate between DOM states (or pages) without complex JS libraries:

function updateUI() {
  document.startViewTransition(() => {
    // Perform DOM modifications
    renderNewPage(); 
  });
}
  • Pseudo-elements: Automatically creates snapshots `::view-transition-group`.
  • Cross-Document: Supported in 2024/2025 for standard multi-page sites.

cards.title: Cascade Layers (@layer)
cards.contentHtml:
Architectural control over specificity, solving the 3rd-party override library wars:

@layer base, utility, components;

@layer utility {
  .p-10 { padding: 10px !important; }
}

@layer base {
  /* Won't be overridden by higher layers 
     despite specificity */
}

cards.title: Relative Color Syntax (RCS)
cards.contentHtml:
Derive dynamic colors natively from existing variables:

:root { --primary: #3498db; }

.btn {
  /* Extract R, G, B and add 50% alpha */
  background: rgb(from var(--primary) r g b / 0.5);
  /* Or oklch adjustments */
  border-color: oklch(from var(--primary) calc(l + 0.1) c h);
}

sections.intro: Core Web Vitals (INP, LCP, CLS), Resource Prioritization, and Interaction to Next Paint.

cards.title: Interaction to Next Paint (INP)
cards.contentHtml:
The modern metric representing total interaction latency. INP tracks every click, tap, and keyboard interaction throughout the entire session:

  • Input Delay: Time between user interaction and event handler start (minimize main thread blocking).
  • Processing Time: Time spent inside the event handler (optimize logic, use `setTimeout(0)` or `isInputPending`).
  • Presentation Delay: Time spent painting the frame (avoid layout thrashing / heavy CSS).

cards.title: Resource Prioritization
cards.contentHtml:
Telling the browser what strictly matters for the Critical Rendering Path:


<img src="hero.avif" 
  fetchpriority="high" 
  loading="eager" 
  decoding="async" 
  alt="LCP Hero">


<script type="speculationrules">
{
  "prerender": [
    { "source": "list", "urls": ["/next-page"] }
  ]
}
</script>

cards.title: Image & Asset Optimization
cards.contentHtml:
Modern formats and strategies for minimizing cumulative layout shifts:

  • AVIF & WebP: Native compression over 50% better than JPEG.
  • Aspect Ratio: Reserve space before images load to prevent layout shifts.
  • Source Set: Use `srcset` and `sizes` to deliver the perfect pixel density for the device.

cards.title: Core Web Vital Thresholds
cards.contentHtml:
Metric Good Poor
LCP (Paint) < 2.5s > 4.0s
INP (Latency) < 200ms > 500ms
CLS (Shift) < 0.1 > 0.25

sections.intro: Every HTML tag a frontend developer must know, organized by category.

cards.title: Text & Inline Semantics
cards.contentHtml:
<p>           <!-- Paragraph -->
<h1> to <h6>  <!-- Headings (one h1 per page, never skip levels) -->
<span>         <!-- Generic inline container (no meaning) -->
<a href>       <!-- Hyperlink (href, target, rel, download) -->
<strong>       <!-- Strong importance (bold + semantic) -->
<em>           <!-- Emphasis (italic + semantic) -->
<b> <i>        <!-- Visual bold/italic ONLY (no semantic meaning) -->
<u>            <!-- Unarticulated annotation (NOT for links!) -->
<s>            <!-- Strikethrough (no longer accurate) -->
<small>        <!-- Fine print, legal text, copyright -->
<sub> <sup>    <!-- Subscript (H₂O) / Superscript (x²) -->
<mark>         <!-- Highlighted text (search results) -->
<del> <ins>    <!-- Tracked deletions/insertions -->
<abbr title>   <!-- Abbreviation with expansion on hover -->
<code>         <!-- Inline code snippet -->
<pre>          <!-- Preformatted text (preserves whitespace) -->
<kbd>          <!-- Keyboard input (Ctrl+C) -->
<samp>         <!-- Sample output from a program -->
<var>          <!-- Variable in math/programming -->
<q>            <!-- Inline quote (auto adds quotation marks) -->
<blockquote>   <!-- Block-level quotation -->
<cite>         <!-- Title of a work (book, film, article) -->
<br>           <!-- Line break (use CSS spacing instead when possible) -->
<wbr>          <!-- Word break opportunity (for long URLs/code) -->
<hr>           <!-- Thematic break (topic change, not just a line) -->
<ruby><rt>     <!-- Pronunciation guide (CJK languages) -->
<bdi><bdo>     <!-- Bidirectional text isolation/override -->
Every one of these has a PURPOSE. Don't use <b> when you mean <strong>. Don't use <br> for spacing (use CSS margin). Don't use <i> when you mean <em>. Semantic elements help screen readers, SEO, and future developers reading your code.

cards.title: Interactive & Embedded Elements
cards.contentHtml:
<button>       <!-- Clickable (type=submit | button | reset) -->
<details>      <!-- Native expandable/collapsible (no JS!) -->
<summary>      <!-- Visible heading for details -->
<dialog>       <!-- Modal/non-modal (.showModal() / .show()) -->
<menu>         <!-- Toolbar or context menu container -->
<output>       <!-- Result of calculation (for= links inputs) -->
<progress>     <!-- Task completion (value/max) -->
<meter>        <!-- Measurement gauge (value/min/max/low/high) -->

MEDIA:
<img>          <!-- Image (ALWAYS include alt, width, height) -->
<picture>      <!-- Responsive image (art direction + formats) -->
<video>        <!-- Video (controls, poster, preload, autoplay) -->
<audio>        <!-- Audio player -->
<source>       <!-- Media source (inside picture/video/audio) -->
<track>        <!-- Subtitles/captions for video/audio -->
<canvas>       <!-- 2D/WebGL drawing surface -->
<svg>          <!-- Scalable vector graphics -->
<iframe>       <!-- Embed another page (sandbox for security) -->
<embed>        <!-- Embed external content -->
<object>       <!-- Embed with fallback content -->
<map><area>    <!-- Image map with clickable regions -->

SCRIPTING:
<script>       <!-- JavaScript (defer | async | type=module) -->
<noscript>     <!-- Fallback when JS disabled -->
<template>     <!-- Inert template (not rendered until cloned) -->
<slot>         <!-- Insertion point in Shadow DOM -->

cards.title: Form Elements — Complete
cards.contentHtml:
<form>         <!-- Form container (action, method, novalidate) -->
<input>        <!-- 22 types: text, email, password, number, tel,
                    url, search, date, time, datetime-local,
                    month, week, color, range, file, hidden,
                    checkbox, radio, submit, reset, button, image -->
<textarea>     <!-- Multi-line text (rows, cols, maxlength) -->
<select>       <!-- Dropdown (multiple for multi-select) -->
<optgroup>     <!-- Group options with label -->
<option>       <!-- Select option (value, selected, disabled) -->
<label>        <!-- Label (for= MUST match input id) -->
<fieldset>     <!-- Group related fields -->
<legend>       <!-- Fieldset caption -->
<datalist>     <!-- Autocomplete suggestions for input -->
<button>       <!-- Submit/reset/button (prefer over input type=submit) -->

VALIDATION ATTRIBUTES:
required       <!-- Must be filled -->
minlength/maxlength  <!-- Text length -->
min/max/step   <!-- Number/date range -->
pattern        <!-- Regex validation -->
autocomplete   <!-- Browser autofill hint (email, name, tel, etc.) -->
inputmode      <!-- Virtual keyboard type (numeric, tel, email) -->
enterkeyhint   <!-- Mobile Enter key label (search, send, go) -->
Always use <button type="button"> for non-submit buttons inside forms. Without type="button", buttons default to type="submit" and will submit the form when clicked!

cards.title: Structural & Semantic — Complete Map
cards.contentHtml:
DOCUMENT:
<html>         <!-- Root (lang= for screen readers + SEO) -->
<head>         <!-- Metadata container -->
<body>         <!-- Visible content -->
<title>        <!-- Page title (50-60 chars, unique per page) -->
<meta>         <!-- Metadata (charset, viewport, description, og:*) -->
<link>         <!-- External resource (CSS, favicon, preload) -->
<style>        <!-- Embedded CSS -->
<base>         <!-- Base URL for relative links (rarely used) -->

SEMANTIC STRUCTURE:
<header>       <!-- Page/section header (logo, nav, search) -->
<nav>          <!-- Navigation links (aria-label if multiple) -->
<main>         <!-- Primary content (ONE per page) -->
<section>      <!-- Thematic grouping WITH a heading -->
<article>      <!-- Self-contained content (blog post, card) -->
<aside>        <!-- Tangentially related (sidebar, ad) -->
<footer>       <!-- Page/section footer (copyright, links) -->
<figure>       <!-- Self-contained media with caption -->
<figcaption>   <!-- Caption for figure -->
<address>      <!-- Contact info for nearest article/body -->
<time>         <!-- Machine-readable date (datetime= attribute) -->
<search>       <!-- Search form container (NEW in 2023) -->
<hgroup>       <!-- Group heading + subtitle -->

LISTS:
<ul>           <!-- Unordered list -->
<ol>           <!-- Ordered list (type, start, reversed) -->
<li>           <!-- List item -->
<dl>           <!-- Description list (glossary, FAQ, metadata) -->
<dt>           <!-- Description term -->
<dd>           <!-- Description detail -->

TABLE:
<table>        <!-- Data table (NOT for layout!) -->
<caption>      <!-- Table title (screen reader announced) -->
<thead>        <!-- Header rows -->
<tbody>        <!-- Body rows -->
<tfoot>        <!-- Footer rows (totals) -->
<tr>           <!-- Table row -->
<th>           <!-- Header cell (scope=col|row) -->
<td>           <!-- Data cell (colspan, rowspan) -->
<colgroup><col> <!-- Column styling -->
Never use <table> for page layout — that's a 1990s practice. Tables are ONLY for tabular data. Use CSS Grid and Flexbox for layout. Screen readers announce tables as data grids, which confuses users when it's actually a page layout.

sections.intro: 52 interview questions from very basic to very advanced. Click to reveal answers.

qa.question: What is semantic HTML and why is it important?
qa.answerHtml:
Using HTML elements that convey the meaning of the content (e.g., `

`, `
`, `
`) instead of generic wrappers (`
`, ``). It severely improves accessibility (for screen readers) and SEO.

qa.question: What is the purpose of the alt attribute on images?
qa.answerHtml: It provides alternative text if the image fails to load, and it is strictly required for screen readers to describe the image to visually impaired users.

qa.question: What is the difference between a block-level element and an inline element?
qa.answerHtml:
Block elements (like `

` or `

`) take up the full width available and start on a new line. Inline elements (like `` or ``) only take up as much width as necessary and do not start on a new line.

qa.question: What does the `defer` attribute do in a `` tag?
qa.answerHtml: It tells the browser to download the script in the background while continuing to parse the HTML, and only execute the script *after* the HTML parsing is completely finished. Prevents render blocking.

qa.question: What is the DOM?
qa.answerHtml: Document Object Model. An object-oriented representation of the webpage, allowing JavaScript to modify the structure, style, and content dynamically.

qa.question: What are `data-` attributes?
qa.answerHtml: Custom attributes (e.g., `data-user-id="123"`) that allow you to store extra information on standard HTML elements without using non-standard attributes. They are easily accessible via JS `element.dataset`.

qa.question: How do you create an accessible form input?
qa.answerHtml: By explicitly associating a `

qa.question: What is an iframe?
qa.answerHtml: Inline Frame. An HTML element used to embed another completely separate HTML document within the current page (often used for videos, maps, or 3rd party ads).

qa.question: What is the difference between `` and ``?
qa.answerHtml: `` simply makes text bold visually. `` makes text bold visually AND implies strong importance/urgency, which screen readers emphasize physically.

qa.question: What is ARIA and when should you use it?
qa.answerHtml: Accessible Rich Internet Applications. It provides attributes (roles, states, properties) to modify semantics for screen readers. Rule of thumb: ONLY use ARIA when native HTML tags cannot achieve the desired behavior.

qa.question: What is the difference between `aria-hidden="true"` and `hidden`?
qa.answerHtml: The HTML `hidden` attribute physically removes the element from the display (like `display: none`). `aria-hidden="true"` leaves the element visible on the screen but hides it completely from screen readers (useful for decorative icons).

qa.question: Explain `tabindex`.
qa.answerHtml: It controls keyboard navigation flow. `tabindex="0"` makes a non-focusable element focusable. `tabindex="-1"` removes an element from sequential keyboard navigation but keeps it focusable via JS. Values > 0 are extremely heavily discouraged as they disrupt expected flow.

qa.question: What is the Shadow DOM?
qa.answerHtml: A web standard that allows attaching a hidden, encapsulated DOM tree to an element. Styles defined inside a Shadow DOM do not leak out, and global styles do not leak in. Crucial for widget isolation.

qa.question: What does `rel="noopener noreferrer"` do?
qa.answerHtml: When opening a link via `target="_blank"`, it prevents the new page from accessing the `window.opener` object (which is a security vulnerability) and strips the Referer header for privacy.

qa.question: How does the browser parse HTML?
qa.answerHtml: It converts raw bytes into characters, then into tokens (tags), then into Nodes, and finally links Nodes into the DOM Tree. Blocking scripts freeze the parser until executed.

qa.question: What is preload, prefetch, and preconnect?
qa.answerHtml: Resource hints. `preconnect`: "I will soon connect to this domain, do DNS/TLS handshake early". `preload`: "I NEED this asset for the current page immediately". `prefetch`: "I MIGHT need this asset for the NEXT page, download it in idle time".

qa.question: What is `aria-live`?
qa.answerHtml: Used to announce dynamic DOM changes (like toast notifications or form errors) to screen readers without shifting focus. Options are `"polite"` (wait until user is idle) or `"assertive"` (interrupt immediately).

qa.question: What is critical rendering path (CRP)?
qa.answerHtml: The sequence of steps the browser takes to convert HTML, CSS, and JS into pixels on the screen. Optimizing CRP involves deferring non-critical scripts, inlining critical CSS, and minimizing layout thrashing.

qa.question: What is the difference between `display: none` and `visibility: hidden`?
qa.answerHtml: `display: none` completely removes the element from the document layout (it takes up zero space). `visibility: hidden` hides the element visually, but it still takes up its precise spatial footprint.

qa.question: What does `box-sizing: border-box` do?
qa.answerHtml: It radically fixes the box model. Instead of padding and borders adding to the element's configured width/height, they are subtracted from it. `width: 100px` with `10px padding` is strictly `100px` total width.

qa.question: How does the `z-index` property work?
qa.answerHtml: It controls the vertical stacking order of elements that overlap. Important: It ONLY works if the element has a `position` value other than `static` (like relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky).

qa.question: What are pseudo-classes vs pseudo-elements?
qa.answerHtml: Pseudo-classes select an element's state (e.g., `:hover`, `:focus`). Pseudo-elements create/target specific virtual parts of an element that aren't in the DOM (e.g., `::before`, `::first-line`).

qa.question: What is `rem` vs `em`?
qa.answerHtml: `rem` is relative strictly to the root `` font-size. `em` is relative to the font-size of the element's direct parent, which can cause chaotic compounding issues if nested deeply.

qa.question: What is a Stacking Context?
qa.answerHtml: A three-dimensional conceptualization of HTML elements. Certain properties (like `opacity < 1`, `transform`, `z-index`, `filter`) create a new local stacking context. Z-indexes inside a stacking context cannot physically break out and overlay elements in a higher parent stacking context.

qa.question: Explain absolute vs relative positioning.
qa.answerHtml: `relative`: The element remains in the normal document flow, but can be visually shifted using top/left. `absolute`: The element is removed completely from normal flow and positioned relative to its closest positioned ancestor (any parent with non-static position).

qa.question: What does `justify-content` do in Flexbox?
qa.answerHtml: It precisely aligns flex items along the main axis (horizontal in `flex-direction: row`).

qa.question: What does `align-items` vs `align-content` do in Flexbox/Grid?
qa.answerHtml: `align-items` aligns items along the cross axis (vertical usually). `align-content` only works when there are MULTIPLE lines of items (like `flex-wrap: wrap`), determining spacing between the lines themselves.

qa.question: What is BEM?
qa.answerHtml: Block, Element, Modifier. A highly scalable CSS naming convention. `.card` (Block), `.card__title` (Element), `.card--dark` (Modifier). It entirely prevents CSS scoping conflicts.

qa.question: How does `calc()` work?
qa.answerHtml: It allows mathematical expressions natively mixing different CSS units. `width: calc(100vw - 50px);`. Invaluable for dynamic geometry.

qa.question: What is Layout Thrashing?
qa.answerHtml: When JS radically toggles between reading the DOM (e.g. `offsetHeight`) and writing to the DOM (altering styles) sequentially inside a loop. This forces the browser to synchronously recalculate layout multiple times, annihilating 60fps performance. Fix: Batch all reads, then batch all writes using `requestAnimationFrame`.

qa.question: What does `will-change` do?
qa.answerHtml: A hint to the browser that an element is about to change (e.g., `will-change: transform`). The browser preemptively allocates GPU layers to it. Overuse consumes massive memory; apply right before animation starts, remove when done.

qa.question: Explain Margin Collapsing.
qa.answerHtml: When the vertical margins of adjoining blocks touch, they combine ("collapse") into a single margin whose size is the largest of the two. This does NOT happen with horizontal margins, or between flex/grid items, or if padding/borders stand between them.

qa.question: What is `content-visibility: auto`?
qa.answerHtml: A monumental performance property. It completely halts the rendering and layout calculations of long, off-screen content sections until the user scrolls near them, dramatically slashing initial page load work.

qa.question: How does the "View Transitions API" essentially work?
qa.answerHtml: It allows creating smooth transitions between different DOM states (or even different pages). When `document.startViewTransition()` is called, the browser captures snapshots of the "old" and "new" states and automatically animates between them using CSS pseudo-elements.

qa.question: What are "Style Queries" in Container Queries?
qa.answerHtml: Unlike size queries that check width/height, style queries (`@container style(--theme: dark)`) allow styling an element based on the computed value of a custom property on its container.

qa.question: What is the "Popover API"?
qa.answerHtml: A native HTML attribute (`popover`) and JS API that provides consistent, accessible behavior for top-layer elements like menus or dialogs. It handles auto-dismiss, backdrop, and stacking order automatically.

qa.question: How does `object-view-box` work?
qa.answerHtml: Similar to SVG's viewBox, it allows you to "crop" a native image element (`` or `

qa.question: How do "Scroll-driven Animations" work natively?
qa.answerHtml: Using `scroll-timeline` or `view-timeline`, CSS animations can be linked to the progress of a scroll container or the visibility of an element within a viewport, enabling complex parallax effects without a single line of JS.

qa.question: Explain the "Light-Dark" color-scheme function.
qa.answerHtml: `color: light-dark(#fff, #000)`. It automatically picks the first color for light mode and the second for dark mode based on the user's system preference or the `color-scheme` property, eliminating the need for complex `@media` blocks.

qa.question: How does `field-sizing: content` work?
qa.answerHtml: A new property for form elements (`

qa.question: What is the "Interpolation-size" property?
qa.answerHtml: Allows animating an element from a fixed size (like `height: 100px`) to an intrinsic size (like `height: auto`), which was previously impossible without JavaScript or grid-template-rows hacks.

qa.question: Explain "Shadow DOM Piercing" selectors.
qa.answerHtml: While `:host` and `::slotted` allow some interaction, some browsers support `::part()` which lets a parent document style specific, exported parts of a shadow tree. The older `/deep/` and `::shadow` selectors are deprecated.

qa.question: How does `accent-color` improve accessibility?
qa.answerHtml: It allows changing the primary brand color for native UI controls (checkboxes, radios, progress bars) with a single line, and the browser automatically ensures the checkmark or foreground color has sufficient contrast against the background.

qa.question: What is "Margin Trim"?
qa.answerHtml: `margin-trim: block` or `inline`. It automatically removes the margin from the last child element of a container that would otherwise cause unwanted overflow or spacing gaps at the end of the box.

qa.question: Explain the "Individual Transform Properties".
qa.answerHtml: Instead of one `transform: scale(2) rotate(45deg)`, browsers now support individual `scale`, `rotate`, and `translate` properties. This allows them to be transitioned and animated independently without overriding each other.

qa.question: Explain "Text Wrap: Balance" vs "Text Wrap: Pretty".
qa.answerHtml: `balance` targets short blocks (like headings) to ensure lines have roughly equal width. `pretty` targets longer paragraphs, specifically trying to prevent "orphans" (a single word on the last line).

qa.question: What is the "Dynamic Viewport Unit" (dvH, dvW)?
qa.answerHtml: Unlike `vh` (which often ignores mobile address bars), `dvh` always represents the *actual* visible height of the viewport as UI elements like the keyboard or URL bar hide and show.

qa.question: How does `inert` attribute work?
qa.answerHtml: A global HTML attribute that tells the browser to completely ignore an element and its subtree for both user interaction (clicks) and accessibility (screen readers/tabs). Ideal for closed drawers or modaly background.

qa.question: Explain "Native Dialog" (``) advantages.
qa.answerHtml: Native focus trapping, auto-dismiss on `Esc`, a high-contrast `::backdrop` pseudo-element, and it always renders in the "Top Layer", meaning it transcends any `overflow: hidden` or `z-index` limits of its parents.

qa.question: What is the "Appearance" property used for?
qa.answerHtml: `appearance: none` removes the default native styling of UI controls (like the dropdown arrow on a `

qa.question: Explain the "Linear-Easing" function.
qa.answerHtml: `easing: linear(0, 0.1, 0.5, 0.9, 1)`. It allows creating custom, complex easing curves (like a spring or "bounce" effect) by defining individual progress points, which was previously only possible with JS.

qa.question: What is "Mix-blend-mode" in depth?
qa.answerHtml: Defines how an element's content should blend with its direct background. Modes like `multiply`, `screen`, and `overlay` allow for complex Photoshop-like visual effects directly in the browser.

qa.question: How do "Search Queries" (`@media (hover: hover)`) help UX?
qa.answerHtml: They allow detecting the user's primary input type. `hover: hover` targets devices with a precise pointer (mouse), whereas `pointer: coarse` targets touch devices, allowing you to increase hit area sizes for mobile.

qa.question: What is `forced-colors` media query?
qa.answerHtml: Crucial for accessibility. It detects if the user has enabled high-contrast mode at the OS level (like Windows High Contrast). Styles inside this block should focus on clarity and use system colors.

qa.question: What is "Interaction to Next Paint" (INP)?
qa.answerHtml: A new Core Web Vital that replaced FID (First Input Delay). It measures the latency of ALL user interactions (clicks, taps, keyboard) during the page life cycle, identifying the longest event processing and rendering delay to ensure responsiveness.

qa.question: How do you optimize for "Largest Contentful Paint" (LCP)?
qa.answerHtml: By prioritizing the "LCP element" (usually a hero image or large text block). Techniques include using `fetchpriority="high"`, preloading the image, using modern formats (avif), and ensuring the server responds quickly (low TTFB).

qa.question: Explain "Cumulative Layout Shift" (CLS) in depth.
qa.answerHtml: It measures the total sum of all unexpected layout shifts that occur during the entire lifespan of the page. Common fixes: reserve space for images/ads using aspect-ratio, use `font-display: optional` to avoid FOIT/FOUT, and never insert content above existing content except in response to a user action.

qa.question: What is "Priority Hints" (`fetchpriority`)?
qa.answerHtml: An attribute that tells the browser the relative importance of a resource. `fetchpriority="high"` on a hero image ensures the browser fetches it before other images, regardless of where it appears in the HTML.

qa.question: How does `decoding="async"` work on images?
qa.answerHtml: It tells the browser that the image decoding process (turning raw data into pixels) can happen off the main thread, preventing the browser from freezing while trying to paint large images.

qa.question: What is "Speculation Rules API"?
qa.answerHtml: A modern way to programmatically tell the browser to pre-render or pre-fetch specific URLs in the background when it's highly likely the user will click them, leading to near-instantaneous page loads.

qa.question: Explain the "Back/forward Cache" (bfcache).
qa.answerHtml: A browser optimization that stores a complete snapshot of a page (including the JS state) in memory when the user navigates away. When they go back, the page is restored instantly from this cache without re-executing scripts or refetching resources.

qa.question: How do you prevent "Layout Thrashing" in modern apps?
qa.answerHtml: By batching DOM reads and writes. Use `requestAnimationFrame` for visual updates and libraries like `fastdom` to orchestrate DOM access. Modern frameworks (React, Vue) handle this automatically via their Virtual DOM / Reactive systems.

qa.question: What is "Resource Timing API"?
qa.answerHtml: A JS API (`performance.getEntriesByType("resource")`) that provides detailed network timing data for every asset loaded. It allows developers to monitor real-world performance of images, scripts, and XHR/fetch calls.

qa.question: Explain "Long Animation Frame API" (LoAF).
qa.answerHtml: A new API that provides much deeper insights into "Long Tasks" than the standard Long Task API. It can pinpoint exactly WHICH script or event handler caused a frame to exceed 50ms, including attribution to the specific source.

qa.question: How does "Content-Visibility" affect A11y?
qa.answerHtml: `content-visibility: auto` hides elements from the rendering tree but NOT necessarily the accessibility tree if used carelessly. Ensure that hidden content doesn't disrupt the screen reader experience or focus management.

qa.question: What is "ARIA-live" assertive vs polite?
qa.answerHtml: `polite`: Screen reader finishes its current sentence before announcing the change. `assertive`: Interrupts the user immediately to announce the change. Use assertive sparingly for critical, time-sensitive errors.

qa.question: Explain "Focus Trapping" logic.
qa.answerHtml: Crucial for modals. It ensures that when a modal is open, the `Tab` and `Shift+Tab` keys cycle focus ONLY between elements INSIDE the modal, preventing the user from accidentally navigating to the background page.

qa.question: What is the "Reduced Motion" media query?
qa.answerHtml: Detects if the user has requested "Reduced Motion" at the OS level. Use it to disable or simplify non-essential animations: `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { ... }`.

qa.question: How does `aria-description` differ from `aria-label`?
qa.answerHtml: `aria-label` provides a primary label (name) for an element. `aria-description` provides supplemental information, similar to a tooltip, which is announced after the label.

qa.question: What is "Focus-visible" pseudo-class?
qa.answerHtml: It allows styling the focus ring ONLY when the user is navigating via keyboard, but hiding it when they are using a mouse. This satisfies accessibility requirements without annoying mouse users.

qa.question: Explain "Accessible Name Computation".
qa.answerHtml: The complex algorithm browsers use to determine what a screen reader should call an element. It checks `aria-labelledby`, then `aria-label`, then native labels, then `alt` / `title`, and finally the inner text.

qa.question: What is "Color Contrast" (WCAG 2.1) requirements?
qa.answerHtml: AA requires a 4.5:1 ratio for normal text and 3:1 for large text. AAA requires 7:1. Modern tools like the Chrome DevTools "CSS Overview" can audit these automatically across a site.

qa.question: How do you handle "Hidden Content" for A11y?
qa.answerHtml: `display: none` hides from everyone. `.visually-hidden` (CSS hack) hides from sighted users but keeps it for screen readers. `aria-hidden="true"` hides from screen readers but keeps it for sighted users.

qa.question: What are "Landmark Roles"?
qa.answerHtml:
ARIA roles like `role="main"`, `role="navigation"`, `role="contentinfo"`. While native elements like `

` and `
` provide these by default, landmarks help screen reader users jump to specific sections of a page quickly.

qa.question: Explain "SVGRIA" (Accessible SVGs).
qa.answerHtml: To make an SVG accessible, add a `` and `<desc>` tag inside it, and reference them with `role="img"` and `aria-labelledby` on the root `<svg>` tag.</p>

qa.question: What is "User-Centric Performance Metrics"?
qa.answerHtml: Moving beyond "Load Time" to metrics that reflect user experience: "Is it happening?" (FCP), "Is it useful?" (LCP), "Is it usable?" (TBT/INP), "Is it delightful?" (CLS).

qa.question: What is "Preload" priority hijacking?
qa.answerHtml: Overusing `` can accidentally delay critical resources because the browser prioritizes all preloads over other initial fetches. Only preload high-importance assets like fonts or the LCP image.

qa.question: How do "Service Workers" improve perceived performance?
qa.answerHtml: By intercepting network requests and serving cached responses instantly. "Stale-while-revalidate" strategies allow the user to see cached content immediately while the worker fetches a fresh version in the background.

qa.question: What is "Gzip" vs "Brotli" compression?
qa.answerHtml: Brotli is a newer compression algorithm designed specifically for the web. It typically achieves 15-20% better compression than Gzip for HTML/CSS/JS files, leading to faster transfer times.

qa.question: Explain "Lazy Loading" on images vs iframes.
qa.answerHtml: Native `loading="lazy"` on both elements defers loading until the user is close to the viewport. For iframes, this is particularly powerful as it prevents loading heavy 3rd-party assets (like YouTube players) until needed.

qa.question: Explain "AVIF" image format benefits.
qa.answerHtml: AVIF offers significantly better compression than WebP and JPEG. It supports 12-bit color, HDR, and transparency, and can often be 50% smaller than a similar quality JPEG.

qa.question: What is "Font Subsetting"?
qa.answerHtml: Creating a custom font file that contains only the specific characters used on your site. This can reduce a 500KB font file to 20KB, greatly improving page load and reducing FOIT.

qa.question: How do "HTTP/3" and QUIC improve performance?
qa.answerHtml: By moving from TCP to UDP-based QUIC, HTTP/3 eliminates "Head-of-Line Blocking". If a single packet is lost, it doesn't freeze all other streams, leading to faster performance on unreliable mobile networks.

qa.question: What is "Early Hints" (HTTP 103)?
qa.answerHtml: An HTTP status code that allows a server to send a partial response to the browser (containing `` headers) while it is still generating the main HTML, allowing the browser to start fetching assets even earlier.

qa.question: What is "Z-index isolation" with `isolation: isolate`?
qa.answerHtml: It creates a new stacking context on an element without needing `position: relative` and `z-index`. This ensures that children elements don't accidentally interact with the z-indexes of elements outside their container.

qa.question: Explain "Variable Fonts" performance impact.
qa.answerHtml: A single font file that contains multiple variations (weights, widths, slants). While the file is larger than a single standard font, it is significantly smaller than loading 6-8 separate files for different weights.

qa.question: How do "Subresource Integrity" (SRI) hashes work?
qa.answerHtml: A security feature that allows browsers to verify that a resource fetched from a CDN (or any 3rd party) hasn't been tampered with. The browser matches the hash in the `integrity` attribute against the actual file content.

qa.question: What is "Mixed Content" security?
qa.answerHtml: When an HTTPS site tries to load assets via HTTP. Browsers block "Active" mixed content (scripts/iframes) entirely and often warn about or upgrade "Passive" mixed content (images/video).

qa.question: Explain "Referrer Policy" in CSS and HTML.
qa.answerHtml: Controls how much referrer information (the URL the user came from) is sent with requests. `strict-origin-when-cross-origin` is the modern secure default.

qa.question: What is "Feature Policy" (Permissions Policy)?
qa.answerHtml: An HTTP header or iframe attribute that allows the site owner to disable specific browser features (like camera, microphone, or geolocation) for their own page or embedded iframes.

qa.question: How does "Content Security Policy" (CSP) prevent XSS?
qa.answerHtml: By explicitly defining which origins and script types are allowed to execute. A strict CSP can block all inline scripts and 3rd-party domains by default, making XSS attacks almost impossible.

qa.question: What is "Prefers-Color-Scheme" in depth?
qa.answerHtml: A media query that lets you respond to the user's OS-level light/dark mode preference. Best practice: use CSS variables to define theme colors and toggle them inside this media query.

qa.question: Explain "Preload" for Dynamic Imports.
qa.answerHtml: Using `` for modules that will be dynamically imported via `import()` later. This ensures the module is already in the cache when the user triggers the interaction requiring it.

qa.question: What is "Aspect-Ratio" property usage?
qa.answerHtml: Allows you to reserve space for an element (like an image or video) before it even loads, preventing layout shifts (CLS). `aspect-ratio: 16 / 9; width: 100%;`.

qa.question: How does `pointer-events: none` work?
qa.answerHtml: It makes an element "invisible" to mouse and touch interactions. Clicks "pass through" it to whatever is behind. Useful for decorative overlays or icons inside buttons.

qa.question: What is "Scroll-Behavior: Smooth"?
qa.answerHtml: A pure CSS way to enable smooth scrolling when a user clicks an anchor link (`#hash`). `html { scroll-behavior: smooth; }`.

qa.question: Explain "Scroll-Margin" for anchor links.
qa.answerHtml: Native property that adds an offset above a target element when it is jumped to via an anchor link. Crucial for fixed headers: `h2 { scroll-margin-top: 100px; }`.

qa.question: What is "Flex-Basis" vs "Width"?
qa.answerHtml: `flex-basis` defines the initial size of a flex item before free space is distributed. `width` is a more rigid constraint. In a flex container, `flex-basis` takes precedence over `width`.

qa.question: What is "Container Units" (cqw, cqh)?
qa.answerHtml: Similar to viewport units (`vw`/`vh`), but relative to the size of the nearest ancestor defined as a container. `10cqw` is 10% of the container's width.

qa.question: How to handle "Modal Dialogue" vs "Alert Dialogue" semantics?
qa.answerHtml: `role="dialog"` is for general interactive popups. `role="alertdialog"` is for critical information that requires a specific user choice (like a deletion confirmation).

qa.question: What is `aria-modal="true"`?
qa.answerHtml: Tells the screen reader that the content below the dialog is not available while the dialog is open, helping them focus exclusively on the modal content.

qa.question: Explain "Aria-Describedby".
qa.answerHtml: An attribute that points to the ID of another element that provides more context for a form input or button. Announced by screen readers after the element's name.

qa.question: What is "Shadow Parts" (`::part`) selector?
qa.answerHtml: Allows a global CSS file to style a specific element inside a Shadow DOM, provided the custom element has exported that portion using the `part` attribute.

qa.question: How do "Web Components" handle accessibility?
qa.answerHtml: Native accessibility features like `tabindex` and `aria-*` attributes work normally. However, developers must take extra care to ensure focus management and aria-references cross the Shadow DOM boundary correctly.

qa.question: What is "Scroll-Padding" in Flex/Grid?
qa.answerHtml: Allows padding the scrollable area itself, which is very useful for ensuring that items in a horizontal scroll gallery aren't snapped flush to the edge of the container.

qa.question: Explain "Grid Areas" logic.
qa.answerHtml: Naming specific regions of a grid: `grid-template-areas: "header header" "nav main"`. This allows for highly semantic and readable layout definitions that are easy to re-arrange in media queries.

qa.question: What is `scrollbar-gutter`?
qa.answerHtml: A property that reserves space for the scrollbar, preventing layout shifts when a page goes from having no scrollbar to having one (or vice versa).

qa.question: How do "Search Engine Optimization" (SEO) and HTML relate?
qa.answerHtml: Beyond semantic tags, SEO relies on correct heading hierarchy (H1-H6), structured metadata (OpenGraph/JSON-LD), and fast performance (Core Web Vitals), which is heavily tied to how HTML/CSS are delivered.

qa.question: What is HTML?
qa.answerHtml: HyperText Markup Language — the standard markup language for web pages. It defines the structure and content of a page using elements (tags). HTML is NOT a programming language — it's a markup language that describes document structure.

qa.question: What is the DOCTYPE declaration?
qa.answerHtml: <!DOCTYPE html> tells the browser to use HTML5 standards mode (not quirks mode). Must be the FIRST line. Without it, browsers may render in quirks mode — inconsistent behavior across browsers.

qa.question: What are semantic HTML elements?
qa.answerHtml: Elements that describe their meaning: <header>, <nav>, <main>, <article>, <section>, <aside>, <footer>, <figure>, <time>, <mark>. They improve accessibility (screen readers announce them), SEO (search engines understand page structure), and code readability.

qa.question: What is the difference between div and span?
qa.answerHtml: <div> is a block-level generic container (takes full width, starts new line). <span> is an inline generic container (only as wide as content, stays in flow). Neither has semantic meaning — use them only when no semantic element fits.

qa.question: What is the difference between block and inline elements?
qa.answerHtml: Block: takes full width, starts new line, can contain block/inline elements (div, p, h1-h6, section). Inline: only as wide as content, stays in text flow, can only contain inline elements (span, a, strong, em, img). Inline-block: inline flow but accepts width/height.

qa.question: What is the alt attribute on images?
qa.answerHtml: Alternative text displayed when image can't load and announced by screen readers. Should describe the image content. Decorative images: alt="" (empty, not missing). Missing alt is an accessibility violation. Also helps SEO (Google Image search).

qa.question: What is the difference between id and class?
qa.answerHtml: id: unique per page (one element), high specificity, used for anchors (href="#section") and JS targeting. class: reusable across many elements, lower specificity, used for styling. One element can have multiple classes but only one id.

qa.question: What are meta tags?
qa.answerHtml: Provide metadata about the page in <head>. charset: character encoding. viewport: mobile responsiveness. description: SEO snippet. robots: crawling instructions. og:*: social sharing preview. They don't appear visually but affect browser behavior and SEO.

qa.question: What is the difference between defer and async on script tags?
qa.answerHtml: defer: downloads in parallel with HTML parsing, executes AFTER HTML is fully parsed, maintains order. async: downloads in parallel, executes IMMEDIATELY when ready (may interrupt parsing), no guaranteed order. Use defer for your app code, async for independent scripts (analytics).

qa.question: What is localStorage vs sessionStorage vs cookies?
qa.answerHtml: localStorage: 5-10MB, persists forever, same-origin, JS only. sessionStorage: 5MB, cleared on tab close, per-tab. Cookies: 4KB, sent with every HTTP request, server-accessible (httpOnly), expiration control. Use cookies for auth tokens (httpOnly), localStorage for preferences, sessionStorage for temporary data.

qa.question: What is the difference between article and section?
qa.answerHtml: <article>: self-contained, independently distributable content (could be extracted and still make sense). Blog post, comment, product card. <section>: thematic grouping of content WITH a heading. Part of a larger piece. Use section for chapters, article for standalone pieces.

qa.question: Explain the native dialog element.
qa.answerHtml: <dialog> provides native modal/non-modal dialogs. .showModal() opens with backdrop, focus trap, Escape-to-close. .close() closes. method="dialog" on forms auto-closes. ::backdrop pseudo-element for overlay. Returns .returnValue. Eliminates need for custom modal libraries in many cases.

qa.question: What is the Popover API?
qa.answerHtml: Native popover/tooltip behavior: <button popovertarget="menu"> + <div id="menu" popover>. Auto: light-dismiss (click outside closes). Manual: explicit open/close only. Handles: positioning, focus management, stacking, Escape key. Reduces JS for dropdowns, menus, tooltips.

qa.question: What are Web Components?
qa.answerHtml: Custom HTML elements with encapsulated markup/styles/behavior. Three technologies: 1) Custom Elements: define new tags with customElements.define(). 2) Shadow DOM: encapsulated DOM tree (styles don't leak in/out). 3) HTML Templates: <template> and <slot> for reusable markup. Framework-agnostic — work everywhere.

qa.question: What is the Content Security Policy (CSP)?
qa.answerHtml: HTTP header that controls what resources can load: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' cdn.example.com; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'. Prevents XSS by blocking inline scripts, unauthorized domains. nonce attribute allows specific inline scripts. Essential security header.

qa.question: Explain srcset and sizes for responsive images.
qa.answerHtml: srcset provides multiple image versions: srcset="small.jpg 320w, medium.jpg 640w, large.jpg 1200w". sizes tells browser how wide the image will display: sizes="(max-width: 640px) 100vw, 50vw". Browser picks the best file based on viewport + DPR. The <picture> element adds art direction (different crops per breakpoint).

qa.question: What is the fetchpriority attribute?
qa.answerHtml: fetchpriority="high" on images tells the browser to prioritize downloading this resource. Use on LCP (hero) images. fetchpriority="low" for below-fold images. Works on <img>, <link>, <script>, and fetch(). Complements loading="lazy" for fine-grained resource control.

qa.question: What are data attributes?
qa.answerHtml: Custom attributes prefixed with data-: <div data-user-id="123" data-role="admin">. Access in JS: element.dataset.userId (camelCase). Access in CSS: [data-role="admin"] { }. Use for: passing data to JS, styling hooks, test selectors. Don't store sensitive data (visible in source).

qa.question: What is ARIA live region?
qa.answerHtml: aria-live="polite|assertive" announces dynamic content changes to screen readers. polite: announces when user is idle (toast notifications). assertive: interrupts immediately (error messages). Add to a container, then update its content — screen reader announces the change. Essential for SPAs where content updates without page reload.

qa.question: How does the browser render a page? (Critical Rendering Path)
qa.answerHtml: 1) Parse HTML → DOM tree. 2) Parse CSS → CSSOM. 3) Combine DOM + CSSOM → Render tree (visible elements only). 4) Layout: calculate geometry (position, size) of every element. 5) Paint: fill pixels (text, colors, images, borders). 6) Composite: combine layers (GPU). CSS blocks rendering. JS blocks HTML parsing. Preload scanner reads ahead. Understanding this pipeline is key to performance optimization.

qa.question: Explain the Shadow DOM in depth.
qa.answerHtml: An encapsulated DOM tree attached to an element. Styles inside don't leak out, external styles don't leak in. Created with element.attachShadow({mode:'open'}). open: accessible via .shadowRoot. closed: not accessible (rare). <slot> projects child content (like ng-content/children). CSS ::part() allows external styling of specific parts. Foundation of Web Components and used by native elements (input, select).

qa.question: What is the View Transitions API?
qa.answerHtml: document.startViewTransition(() => updateDOM()) creates smooth transitions between DOM states. The browser: 1) Screenshots old state. 2) Runs your DOM update. 3) Screenshots new state. 4) Cross-fades between them. CSS pseudo-elements ::view-transition-old and ::view-transition-new for custom animations. Works with MPA navigations in Chromium. Eliminates need for complex JS animation libraries for page transitions.

qa.question: What are the differences between Service Workers, Web Workers, and Shared Workers?
qa.answerHtml: Web Worker: Background thread for heavy computation. Private to one tab. new Worker(). Shared Worker: Shared across all tabs of same origin. new SharedWorker(). Communication via ports. Service Worker: Proxy between app and network. Intercepts fetch requests for offline caching, push notifications, background sync. Registered with navigator.serviceWorker.register(). Has lifecycle (install, activate, fetch events).

qa.question: Explain the Intersection Observer API.
qa.answerHtml: Asynchronously observes visibility of elements relative to viewport or ancestor. new IntersectionObserver(callback, { root, rootMargin, threshold }). callback receives entries with isIntersecting, intersectionRatio. Uses: lazy loading images, infinite scroll triggers, scroll-based animations, impression tracking, sticky header detection. Replaces scroll event listeners (much more performant).

qa.question: How do you implement an accessible modal?
qa.answerHtml: 1) Use <dialog> element with .showModal(). 2) Focus trap: first focusable element gets focus on open, Tab cycles within modal. 3) aria-modal="true", role="dialog", aria-labelledby pointing to heading. 4) Close on Escape key. 5) inert attribute on background content. 6) Return focus to trigger element on close. 7) Prevent body scroll. The native dialog handles most of this automatically.

qa.question: What is the difference between section, article, and div?
qa.answerHtml: section: thematic grouping WITH a heading (chapters, tabbed content). article: self-contained content that makes sense independently (blog post, comment, product card). div: no semantic meaning, just a generic container for styling/scripting.

qa.question: What is the purpose of the head element?
qa.answerHtml: Contains metadata about the document — not displayed to users. Includes: <title>, <meta> (charset, viewport, description), <link> (stylesheets, favicon, preload), <script>, <style>, Open Graph tags, and structured data.

qa.question: What is the viewport meta tag?
qa.answerHtml: <meta name='viewport' content='width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0'> tells mobile browsers to: set the viewport width to the device width (not a default 980px), set initial zoom to 100%. Without it, mobile browsers render a desktop-width page scaled down. Essential for responsive design.

qa.question: What are void/self-closing elements?
qa.answerHtml: Elements that cannot have children and don't need closing tags: <img>, <input>, <br>, <hr>, <meta>, <link>, <source>, <area>, <col>, <embed>, <wbr>. The trailing slash (<img />) is optional in HTML5 but required in JSX/React.

qa.question: What is the difference between strong/em and b/i?
qa.answerHtml: <strong>: semantic importance (screen readers emphasize). <b>: visually bold, no semantic meaning. <em>: semantic emphasis (screen readers change tone). <i>: visually italic, no emphasis. Prefer strong/em for meaning, b/i only for stylistic purposes.

qa.question: What is the target attribute on anchor tags?
qa.answerHtml: _self: same tab (default). _blank: new tab/window (add rel='noopener noreferrer' for security). _parent: parent frame. _top: full window. _blank without noopener gives the new page access to window.opener (security risk).

qa.question: What is the picture element?
qa.answerHtml: Container for multiple <source> elements for responsive images. Enables: art direction (different crops per screen size), format selection (AVIF → WebP → JPEG fallback), and media-query-based image switching. The browser picks the first matching source. The <img> inside is the fallback.

qa.question: What is the difference between preload, prefetch, and preconnect?
qa.answerHtml: preload: download this resource NOW, it's needed for current page (fonts, critical CSS/JS). prefetch: download this resource later, it's needed for NEXT navigation (next page's JS). preconnect: establish connection to origin early (DNS + TCP + TLS). dns-prefetch: only DNS lookup.

qa.question: What is the inert attribute?
qa.answerHtml: Makes an element and all descendants non-interactive and invisible to assistive tech. <div inert>...</div>. Use for: background content when modal is open, disabled sections. The element is not focusable, not clickable, not announced by screen readers. Much better than aria-hidden + tabindex=-1 on every child.

qa.question: What are ARIA landmarks?
qa.answerHtml: Regions of a page that assistive tech can navigate to directly: banner (header), navigation (nav), main (main), complementary (aside), contentinfo (footer), search, form. Semantic HTML elements automatically create landmarks: <header> = banner, <nav> = navigation. Screen readers let users jump between landmarks with keyboard shortcuts.

qa.question: What is progressive enhancement?
qa.answerHtml: Build a baseline functional experience for all browsers/devices, then enhance with advanced features for capable ones. HTML (structure) → CSS (presentation) → JS (interactivity). Example: a form works with plain HTML (submit to server), enhanced with JS (AJAX submit, validation, animations). Opposite of graceful degradation.

qa.question: What is the Mutation Observer API?
qa.answerHtml: Watches for changes to the DOM tree: additions/removals of child elements, attribute changes, text content changes. new MutationObserver(callback).observe(target, { childList: true, attributes: true, subtree: true }). Replaces deprecated Mutation Events. Uses: detecting third-party DOM changes, auto-initializing widgets, content change tracking. More performant than polling.

qa.question: What is the Performance API?
qa.answerHtml: Measures page and resource performance. performance.now(): high-resolution timestamp. performance.mark('start') + performance.measure('duration', 'start'): custom timing. PerformanceObserver: observe LCP, FID, CLS, Long Tasks. performance.getEntriesByType('resource'): all resource load times. Foundation for Real User Monitoring (RUM).

qa.question: What is the difference between Canvas and SVG?
qa.answerHtml: Canvas: Bitmap/raster, immediate mode (draw and forget), pixel manipulation, better for: complex scenes, games, image processing, many objects. SVG: Vector, retained mode (DOM elements), scalable without blur, accessible (text in SVG is readable), better for: icons, charts, logos, animations, interactive graphics. Canvas: JS-driven. SVG: CSS-animatable, DOM-queryable.

qa.question: What are Web Vitals and how do you measure them in code?
qa.answerHtml: Use the web-vitals library: import {onLCP, onINP, onCLS} from 'web-vitals'. Each function takes a callback with the metric value. Send to analytics. PerformanceObserver for custom measurement: new PerformanceObserver(list => list.getEntries().forEach(entry => ...)) with type: 'largest-contentful-paint'. Automate with Lighthouse CI in your pipeline.

qa.question: What is the difference between link and @import for CSS?
qa.answerHtml: <link> in HTML: loads CSS in parallel, non-blocking (with media attribute), recommended. @import in CSS: sequential loading (blocks), can't be conditional, creates waterfall. Always use <link> — @import adds extra round trips and slows rendering.

qa.question: What is the autocomplete attribute?
qa.answerHtml: Tells browser what data to auto-fill: autocomplete='email', 'name', 'tel', 'address-line1', 'cc-number', 'new-password', 'current-password'. Massive UX win — users fill forms 30% faster. Required for WCAG 1.3.5 compliance. Set autocomplete='off' to disable (use sparingly).

qa.question: What is the difference between absolute and relative URL paths?
qa.answerHtml: Absolute: https://example.com/page — full URL. Protocol-relative: //example.com/page — matches current protocol (deprecated). Root-relative: /page — relative to domain root. Relative: ./page or ../page — relative to current file. Use root-relative for internal links, absolute for external.

qa.question: What is the loading attribute?
qa.answerHtml: loading='lazy' defers loading until near viewport (images, iframes). loading='eager' loads immediately (default). Use lazy for below-fold content, eager (or omit) for above-fold. Works natively in all modern browsers — no IntersectionObserver needed for basic lazy loading. Combine with fetchpriority for fine control.

qa.question: What is the dialog element and how does it differ from a div modal?
qa.answerHtml: <dialog> provides: native backdrop (::backdrop), focus trapping (Tab stays inside), Escape to close, inert on background, proper stacking above all content. A <div> modal needs ALL of this manually — focus trap, scroll lock, Escape handler, backdrop, z-index battles. Use native dialog and save 100+ lines of JS.

qa.question: What is the contenteditable attribute?
qa.answerHtml: contenteditable='true' makes any element editable by the user. Used as the foundation for rich text editors (with execCommand or modern document.execCommand replacement). Gotchas: inconsistent behavior across browsers, XSS risk if saving user input, difficult to control formatting. Modern editors use it with sanitization layers.

qa.question: What are CORS and how does it relate to HTML?
qa.answerHtml: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing affects HTML loading: crossorigin attribute on <script>, <link>, <img> controls CORS mode. crossorigin='anonymous': no credentials sent. crossorigin='use-credentials': cookies sent. Required for: preloaded fonts, canvas tainted images, error details for cross-origin scripts.

qa.question: What is the picture element vs img srcset?
qa.answerHtml: <img srcset>: resolution switching — browser picks best size for same image. <picture>: art direction — serve different images per breakpoint (different crops, orientations). Also for format fallback: <source type='image/avif'> → <source type='image/webp'> → <img src='fallback.jpg'>.

qa.question: How does the browser's preload scanner work?
qa.answerHtml: When the main HTML parser is blocked (by CSS or sync JS), a lightweight secondary scanner reads ahead to discover resources (images, scripts, stylesheets) and starts downloading them in parallel. This is why <link rel='stylesheet'> in the head doesn't completely block resource discovery. It's also why dynamically injected scripts miss this optimization — add resource hints (preload) for critical dynamic resources.

qa.question: What is the Speculation Rules API?
qa.answerHtml: Declarative prefetching/prerendering for faster navigation: <script type='speculationrules'>{"prerender":[{"where":{"href_matches":"/blog/*"}}]}</script>. The browser preloads or even fully renders pages the user might navigate to next. More efficient than <link rel='prefetch'> because it uses document rules and eagerness levels. Supported in Chromium browsers.

qa.question: What is declarative Shadow DOM?
qa.answerHtml: <template shadowrootmode='open'> creates Shadow DOM without JavaScript — works with SSR. The server sends the shadow root as HTML, the browser attaches it as a shadow tree. Enables Web Components to be server-rendered. Without it, Web Components were client-only (required JS to attachShadow()).

qa.question: How do you implement a skip link?
qa.answerHtml: <a href='#main' class='skip-link'>Skip to content</a> — first element in body, visually hidden until focused, jumps to <main id='main'>. CSS: .skip-link { position: absolute; transform: translateY(-100%); } .skip-link:focus { transform: translateY(0); }. Essential for keyboard users to bypass repetitive navigation. WCAG 2.4.1 requirement.
